65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85	
86	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
87	
88	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
89	
90	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
91	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
92	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
93	
94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
115	
116	## Logging
117	
118	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
119	
120	```markdown
121	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
122	
123	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
124	### Attempt 1
125	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
126	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
127	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
128	
129	### Attempt 2
130	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
131	- **Result:** RESOLVED
132	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
133	
134	## helper_bijective (line 78)
135	### Attempt 1
136	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
137	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
138	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
139	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
140	
141	## Needs blueprint entry
142	- `Algebra.WLocal.helper_bijective` (line 78) — new helper, no blueprint block yet. Uses: `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`. Reviewer/planner: add a `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses` entry so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds.
143	```
144	
145	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter. **Always include the `## Needs blueprint entry` section when you added any new declaration** (omit it only if you added none) — it is how the loop keeps Lean ↔ blueprint at 1-to-1.
146	
147	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
148	
149	## End-of-session handoff
150	
151	Before stopping:
152	
153	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
154	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
352	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
353	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
354	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
355	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
357	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
358	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
359	  %  function
360	  %  $$
361	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
362	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
363	  %  $$
364	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
365	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
366	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
367	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
368	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
369	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
370	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
371	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
372	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
373	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
374	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
375	  such that for all \(n > N\)
376	  \[
377	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
378	  \]
379	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
380	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
381	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
382	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
383	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
384	
385	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
386	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
387	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
388	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
389	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
390	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
391	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
392	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
393	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
394	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
395	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
396	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
397	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
398	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
399	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
400	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
404	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
405	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
406	  %   and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration; the ambient
407	  %   subquotient route (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing
408	  %   graded-quotient API by working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
412	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
413	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
414	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
415	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
416	%  following short exact sequence
417	%  $$
418	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
419	%  $$
420	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
421	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
422	\begin{proof}
423	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
424	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
425	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
426	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
427	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
428	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
429	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
430	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
431	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
432	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
433	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
434	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
435	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
436	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
437	
438	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
439	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
440	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
441	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
442	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
443	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
444	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
445	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
446	  is the number on which we induct.
447	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
448	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
449	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
450	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
451	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
452	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
453	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
454	
455	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
456	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
457	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
458	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
459	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
460	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
461	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
462	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
463	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
464	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
465	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
466	
467	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
468	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
469	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
470	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
471	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
472	  \[
473	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
474	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
475	  \]
476	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
477	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
478	  with
479	  \[
480	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
481	    \qquad (n > N),
482	  \]
483	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
484	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
485	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
486	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
487	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
488	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
489	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
490	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
491	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
492	\end{proof}
493	
494	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
495	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
496	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
497	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
498	
499	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
500	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
501	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
502	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
503	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
504	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
505	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
506	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
507	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
508	
509	\begin{lemma}\leanok
510	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
511	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
513	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
514	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
515	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
516	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
517	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
518	  \(F\):
519	  \[
520	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
521	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
522	  \]
523	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
524	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
525	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
526	  the Cauchy product.
527	\end{lemma}
528	
529	\begin{lemma}\leanok
530	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
531	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
533	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
534	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
535	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
536	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
537	  \[
538	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
539	  \]
540	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
541	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
542	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
543	  \[
544	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
545	  \]
546	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
547	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
548	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
549	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
550	\end{lemma}
551	
552	\begin{proof}
553	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
554	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
555	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
556	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
557	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
558	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
559	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
560	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
561	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
562	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
563	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
564	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
565	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
566	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
567	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
568	\end{proof}
569	
570	\begin{definition}\leanok
571	[Rational Hilbert function]
572	  \label{def:ratHilb}
573	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
574	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
575	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
576	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
577	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
578	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
579	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
580	  \[
581	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
582	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
583	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
584	  \]
585	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
586	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
587	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
588	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
589	\end{definition}
590	
591	\begin{lemma}\leanok
592	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
593	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
595	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
596	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
597	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
598	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
599	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
600	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
601	\end{lemma}
602	
603	\begin{lemma}\leanok
604	[Raising the pole order]
605	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
607	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
608	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
609	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
610	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
611	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
612	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
613	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
614	\end{lemma}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}\leanok
617	[Closure under difference]
618	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
620	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
621	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
622	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
623	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
624	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
625	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
626	\end{lemma}
627	
628	\begin{lemma}\leanok
629	[Closure under right shift]
630	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
631	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
632	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
633	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
634	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
635	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
636	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
637	  Lean.
638	\end{lemma}
639	
640	\begin{lemma}\leanok
641	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
642	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
644	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
645	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
646	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
647	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
648	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
649	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
650	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
651	\end{lemma}
652	
653	\begin{proof}
654	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
655	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
656	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
657	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
658	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
659	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
660	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
661	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
662	\end{proof}
663	
664	\begin{lemma}\leanok
665	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
666	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
668	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
669	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
670	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
671	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
672	  \[
673	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
674	  \]
675	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
676	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
677	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
678	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
679	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
680	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
681	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
682	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
683	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
684	\end{lemma}
685	
686	\begin{proof}
687	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
688	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
689	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
690	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
691	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
692	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
693	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
694	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
695	\end{proof}

697	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
698	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
699	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
700	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
701	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
702	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
703	  % Polynomials" (read from
704	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
705	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
706	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
707	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
708	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
709	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
710	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
711	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
712	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
713	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
714	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
715	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
716	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
717	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
718	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
719	  \[
720	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
721	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
722	  \]
723	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
724	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
725	\end{theorem}
726	
727	\begin{proof}
728	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
729	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
730	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
731	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
732	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
733	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
734	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
735	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
736	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
737	  function
738	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
739	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
740	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
741	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
742	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
743	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
744	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
745	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
746	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
747	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
748	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
749	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
750	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
751	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
752	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
753	\end{proof}

755	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
756	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
757	
758	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
759	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
760	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
761	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
762	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
763	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
764	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
765	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
766	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
767	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
768	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
769	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
770	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
771	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
772	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
773	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
774	
775	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
Let me continue reading the blueprint (subquotient induction section) and look at my Lean file.

776	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
777	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
778	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
779	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
780	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
781	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
782	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
783	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
784	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
785	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
786	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
787	
788	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
789	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
790	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
791	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
792	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
793	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
794	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
795	\(x \in R_1\).
796	
797	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
800	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
801	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
802	  \mathlibok
803	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
804	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
805	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
806	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
807	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
808	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
809	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
810	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
811	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
812	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
813	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
814	\end{lemma}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
817	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
818	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
819	  \mathlibok
820	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
821	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
822	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
823	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
824	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
825	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
826	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
827	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
828	\end{lemma}

830	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
831	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
832	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
833	  \mathlibok
834	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
835	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
836	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
837	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
838	  \[
839	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
840	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
841	  \]
842	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
843	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).

846	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
847	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
848	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
849	  \mathlibok
850	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
851	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
852	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
